title: Hardness of Randomized Truthful Mechanisms for Combinatorial Auctions
speaker: Jan Vondrák
abstract: The problem of combinatorial auctions is one of the basic questions in algorithmic mechanism design: how can we allocate/sell m items to n agents with private valuations of different combinations of items, so that the agents are motivated to reveal their true valuations and the outcome is (at least approximately) optimal in terms of total utility? While approximation algorithms exist for several non-trivial classes of valuations, they typically do not motivate agents to report truthfully.

proof_stages.label: Combinatorial-auction model and truthfulness notions
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up agents, items, allocations, payments, social welfare, computational efficiency, and the deterministic, universally truthful, and truthful-in-expectation incentive constraints.

proof_stages.label: Baselines, valuation classes, and prior landscape
proof_stages.summary: VCG is identified as the exact truthful benchmark, approximation becomes necessary because welfare maximization is hard, monotone submodular and related valuation classes are introduced, and prior positive and negative results are reviewed.

proof_stages.label: Main lower-bound statements and proof roadmap
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states the new oracle lower bound for truthful-in-expectation mechanisms for submodular valuations and its conversion to succinct computational hardness, then outlines the tools: symmetry gaps, distributional range arguments, taxation, and list-decodable codes.

proof_stages.label: Coverage-valuations positive mechanism via concave relaxation
proof_stages.summary: The Dugmi–Roughgarden–Yan mechanism for coverage valuations is explained by replacing discrete allocations with distributions/fractional allocations, optimizing a specially chosen concave extension, and applying VCG payments to obtain truthfulness in expectation.

proof_stages.label: Symmetry-gap obstruction for submodular extensions
proof_stages.summary: The coverage relaxation fails for general submodular valuations because analogous extensions need not be concave, and a hidden two-sided symmetric construction makes oracle queries see only the inferior balanced region rather than the asymmetric optima.

proof_stages.label: Inductive range blow-up for VCG-based mechanisms
proof_stages.summary: For mechanisms optimizing over a fixed range of distributions, the symmetry-gap construction is iterated over a hierarchy of partitions, forcing any sufficiently approximating range to contain distributions allocating more and more items until it exceeds the instance size.

proof_stages.label: Taxation-principle reduction to arbitrary truthful mechanisms
proof_stages.summary: Using Dobzinski’s taxation-principle framework, fixing other players turns a truthful mechanism into a single-player menu/pricing maximization problem, and the prior hardness machinery is adapted to handle arbitrary price menus via scaling/Farkas-type arguments.

proof_stages.label: List-decodable-code translation to computational hardness
proof_stages.summary: The hidden partition that drives the oracle lower bound is encoded as a codeword of a unique satisfying assignment, with list decoding allowing valuation evaluation while preserving hardness, thereby yielding succinctly represented submodular valuations hard for truthful mechanisms unless NP is contained in P/poly.

examples.name: Single-item second-price auction
examples.note: The familiar one-item auction illustrates the three desiderata—truthfulness, efficient computation, and welfare optimality—before the multi-item setting is introduced.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker runs through the single-item second-price auction, describing bids, allocation to the highest bidder, charging the second price, and checking truthfulness, efficiency, and social optimality.

passes.reason: The one-item Vickrey auction is the minimal boundary case of combinatorial auctions and is used as a positive benchmark, not a failure case.
minimal one-item auction case where the second-price rule exactly implements the welfare-optimal truthful outcome
An anonymous one-item base case that exactly maximizes welfare, used as the positive truthful benchmark.
The Vickrey auction is the minimal one-item base case and is welfare-optimal/truthful in that setting, used only illustratively rather than to refute a claim.
the Vickrey auction is the anonymous, fully regular mechanism for the minimal one-item base case, used as a positive illustration
It is the minimal one-item auction case and the Vickrey rule gives the welfare-maximizing allocation rather than refuting a claim.
The one-item Vickrey auction is a minimal auction setting that exactly attains truthful, efficient, welfare-optimal allocation rather than refuting a claim.
Used as the solvable baseline; the one-item case is a minimal combinatorial-auction case and the mechanism exactly attains truthful welfare maximization.
the one-item Vickrey auction is the minimal combinatorial-auction base case and exactly attains welfare optimality
the one-item second-price auction is an anonymous minimal auction case that exactly attains truthful welfare-optimal allocation
Used as the positive one-item base case of truthful efficient auctions, not to refute a claim.
it is the minimal one-item base case used as a positive illustration of truthful efficient auctions

examples.name: Budget-additive valuations
examples.note: The valuation min of an additive value and a budget cap gives a concrete subclass of monotone submodular valuations used to situate known positive and negative results.
examples.engagement_reason: Budget-additive valuations are defined as additive values capped by a budget and noted to be submodular, but no concrete construction or calculation is performed on them.

passes.reason: Budget-additive valuations are a natural capped-additive subclass, used in part as a restricted setting where VCG-based mechanisms still face strong lower bounds.
a systematic subclass of monotone submodular valuations of the form min(budget, additive value), introduced as a representative restricted valuation class
A natural submodular valuation subclass also cited as a simple domain where VCG-based mechanisms still face hardness.
Budget-additive valuations are a systematic subclass of submodular valuations with arbitrary weights and a cap, introduced as a representative valuation class rather than a counterexample.
budget-additive valuations are a standard restricted subclass of monotone submodular valuations, introduced as a model class
Budget-additive valuations are a collapsed capped-additive subclass of submodular valuations, introduced as a natural restricted class rather than a refutation.
Budget-additive valuations are presented as a natural simple subclass of submodular valuations used in ad-auction models, not as a sporadic or limiting object.
A natural subclass of monotone submodular valuations used as a restricted hard setting where VCG-based mechanisms still fail to get good approximation.
budget-additive valuations are cited as a simple monotone-submodular subclass used in ad-auction models
budget-additive valuations are used as a natural succinct subclass of monotone submodular valuations, not as an obstruction

examples.name: Maximum coverage over k sets and the 1-1/e barrier
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker compares the coverage-valuation mechanism’s optimal factor with Feige’s hardness threshold for maximum coverage.
examples.engagement_reason: Maximum coverage over k sets and the 1-1/e barrier are invoked as the source of an analogous optimality/hardness threshold, without deriving or working through the example.

passes.reason: Maximum coverage is invoked through Feige's tight 1-1/e approximation barrier, obstructing any better general approximation.

examples.name: Nonmonotone submodular maximization generalizing Max Cut

examples.engagement_reason: Nonmonotone submodular maximization generalizing Max Cut is only named at the end as an application of the hardness framework, with no worked instance or proof.
